summary:
The invention of eyeglasses (c. 1286–1290 AD) in northern Italy — attributed by various sources to the glassmakers of Venice or Pisa, with the earliest convincing historical reference in a 1306 sermon by Fra Giordano da Pisa — was one of the most practically important technological innovations of the medieval period, dramatically extending the productive intellectual and working lives of people with vision problems and enabling the sustained reading and close work that the expanding literate culture of the late medieval and Renaissance periods required. The first eyeglasses were convex lenses for farsightedness (presbyopia); concave lenses for nearsightedness followed in the 15th century.

The social and intellectual consequences of eyeglasses were profound and underappreciated. Before eyeglasses, presbyopia — the normal farsightedness of aging that makes close work impossible — typically disabled scholars, scribes, craftsmen, and skilled artisans from their primary work by their mid-40s. Eyeglasses doubled or tripled the productive intellectual careers of literate Europeans, enabling scholars to continue reading, writing, and teaching through their 60s and 70s rather than ceasing close work in their 40s. This extended the careers of exactly those people — scholars, lawyers, administrators — whose accumulated knowledge and judgment were most valuable to medieval society. The development of eyeglasses also required and stimulated advances in glass-grinding technology that contributed to the eventual development of the microscope and telescope in the 17th century, making spectacles an indirect contributor to the Scientific Revolution. By 1400, eyeglasses were sufficiently common in Italy that they appeared routinely in portraits of scholars and churchmen.
